text: We took the patient to the operating room.
and perform a right brachial thromboembolectomy by making an incision proximal to the antecubital crease on the medial aspect of the upper arm.
Dissection was carried through the skin and subcutaneous tissue.
Bicep brachii muscle was then identified as demonstrated here.
We continued our dissection medial to the bicep brachii muscle.
We identified the brachial artery and then dissected free from the surrounding tissue as demonstrated in this video.
Vessel loops were placed distally and proximally on the brachial artery.
The brachial artery was found to be healthy in this location without any evidence of thrombus as determined from the prior duplex performed.
The patient was given angiomex at this point in time and an arteriotomy made in the brachial artery using an 11 blade.
Next, a forefrench thrombectomy catheter was introduced into the proximal brachial artery and we performed our thromboembolectomy using this forefrench Fogarty catheter.
The balloon is inflated and the catheter is gently pulled back.
We retrieve a significant amount of thrombus from the brachial artery as demonstrated in this video.
Shown here is the brachial artery thrombus.
Brace forward bleeding was then identified after the thromboembolectomy.
We also had decent back bleeding from the distal brachial artery.
However, we decided to perform a distal brachial artery thromboembolectomy and had good back bleeding from the artery.
Next, we introduced a 9-franc sheet through this brachial artery to perform a right upper extremity angiogram.
we advanced the sheath in the brachial artery and then performed our right upper extremity angiogram that demonstrated no evidence of any thrombus in the axillary or brachial artery.

Once this angiogram was completed, the sheath was gently retracted from the brachial artery and at this point in time, we noticed brisk bleeding from the proximal brachial artery as demonstrated here.
The arteriotomy in the brachial artery was then primarily repaired using a 6 of proline suture.
Prior to the completion of our anastomosis, the distal brachial artery clamp is removed, followed by the proximal clamp.
Good back and forward breathing was noticed.
Patient had a palpable brachial pulse and good radian and ulnar signals at the end of the case.
